name: Hermod
description: Hermod, often called Hermóðr the Bold or Hermod the Swift, is a figure in Norse mythology, primarily known for his role as a messenger of the gods and his journey to Hel to retrieve Baldr. He is generally considered one of Odin's sons, although some sources leave his parentage ambiguous. Hermod is depicted as a brave and agile god, possessing exceptional speed, which allows him to travel between the realms of Asgard, Midgard, and Hel with remarkable ease. His most significant feat is undoubtedly his ride to Hel on Sleipnir, Odin's eight-legged horse, to plead with Hel, the ruler of the underworld, for Baldr's release. He successfully negotiated with Hel, but ultimately failed due to Loki's interference, demonstrating both his courage and the limitations of even divine power against fate. While not as prominent as other major Norse gods like Thor or Odin, Hermod's role as a messenger and his daring journey to Hel highlight his importance in the divine order and the interconnectedness of the Norse cosmos. He represents loyalty, speed, and the courage to undertake perilous missions on behalf of his fellow gods. His story underscores the themes of fate, grief, and the struggle against insurmountable odds that are central to Norse mythology. He is sometimes associated with diplomacy and communication, given his messenger role.
subtitle: Swift Messenger of the Gods

keyMyths.title: Hermod's Ride to Hel
keyMyths.description: Hermod rides Sleipnir to Hel to plead for Baldr's return, showcasing his courage and speed. He negotiates with Hel but fails due to Loki's trickery.

keyMyths.title: Messenger of the Gods
keyMyths.description: Hermod serves as a messenger, conveying information and requests between the gods and other beings. This role highlights his importance in the divine order.

keyMyths.title: Son of Odin (Debated)
keyMyths.description: While often considered a son of Odin, some sources are ambiguous about Hermod's parentage. His connection to Odin, however, is generally accepted.

sources.author: Snorri Sturluson
